mūla.devanāgarī: सर्वस्य चाहं हृदि संनिविष्टो मत्तः स्मृतिर् ज्ञानम् अपोहनं च | वेदैश् च सर्वैर् अहम् एव वेद्यो वेदान्त-कृद् वेद-विद् एव चाहम्
mūla.iast: sarvasya cāhaṃ hṛdi saṃniviṣṭo mattaḥ smṛtir jñānam apohanaṃ ca | vedaiś ca sarvair aham eva vedyo vedānta-kṛd veda-vid eva cāham
mūla.chapter_position: Chapter 15 (Puruṣottama-Yoga (The Yoga of the Supreme Person)), verse 15
mūla.speaker: Krishna

advaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita.english_rendering: I, as the innermost Atman (self), am fully lodged in the buddhi (intellect) of every being. It is from Me alone that memory and knowledge arise in those whose past karma (action) is meritorious, and it is from Me that their cessation occurs in those weighed down by demerit. By all the Vedas I alone — the Paramatman (supreme self) — am to be known; I am the one who established the Vedanta tradition, and I alone truly know the Veda's meaning.
advaita.divergence_note: Shankara: 'sarvasya pranijatasya aham atma san hrdi buddhau sannivishtah... yathapunyakarmanam... smriti-jnane bhavatah, papakarmishu apohananam ca' — memory and forgetting are karma-apportioned distributions of the single Atman's light, not independent faculties.

viśiṣṭādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
viśiṣṭādvaita.english_rendering: I, controlling all things by My own sankalpa (will), dwell as the inner ruler (antaryamin) in the heart — the seat where all knowledge for activity and cessation arises — in every being. From Me alone springs memory (recollection of prior experience), determinate knowledge (vijnana born of sense-contact), and inference-supporting reasoning (uha); and their cessation too is from Me. All the Vedas, even those speaking of Indra, Varuna and the rest, know Me alone as their inner content, for all those deities have Me as their inner controller. I am the bestower of all Vedic fruits and the sole knower of the Veda's true intent.
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence_note: Ramanuja quotes Brhadaranyaka Upanishad 3.7 ('yah prthivyam tisthan... yamayati') and Vishnu Purana 1.17.20 to ground the antaryamin claim; smriti, jnana, and uha are three epistemically distinct operations all sourced in the Lord's governance.

dvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
dvaita.english_rendering: The Vedanta — the Mimamsa that determines the Veda's meaning — and I are not separate: the Samaveda itself declares 'sa vedantakrt sa kalakah,' identifying the author of the Vedanta-sutra with the supreme Lord. Memory, knowledge, and their removal are dispensed by Me in strict dependence; the jiva (individual soul), wholly distinct from Me, receives or loses these faculties solely as I ordain.
dvaita.divergence_note: Madhva cites the Prachinashala shruti from the Samaveda: 'sa vedantakrt sa kalakah — he is the maker of the Vedanta, he is Kalaka (the supreme),' grounding his identification of the Vedanta-sutra's authorship with Vishnu/Hari. Bhashya on the jiva-Brahman distinction is minimal here; this verse is primarily used for the Vedanta-authorship thesis.

śuddhādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
śuddhādvaita.english_rendering: It is I who, dwelling in every being's heart as their very Atman and controlling all by My own sankalpa, am the source of memory, determinate knowledge, and even the inferential reasoning (uha) that tests whether a means of knowledge is fit to operate — and their removal too is from Me alone. All Vedas, even those that seemingly speak of fire, soma, or wind, speak only of Me as their inner substance. I, as Krishnadvaipayanacharya (Vyasa), authored the Vedanta and its tradition; no other knows the Veda's true meaning. The world's apparent superimposition is the jiva's construction, not Mine; My maya is not a separate tattva (principle) but My own power — the Brahma-vada absorbs even this.
śuddhādvaita.divergence_note: Vallabha follows Ramanuja's three-fold epistemology (smriti / jnana / uha) closely, then turns polemical: apohana as jiva-kalpita (jiva-constructed) superimposition places the burden of maya on the jiva, not Brahman — his counter to Advaita's vivartavada. The Brahma-vada framing ('jagato alikatvasya avacaniyatvat') is explicit in the bhashya.

bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
bhakti.english_rendering: I am fully entered into the heart of every being as the antaryamin (inner ruler). From Me alone arises in every creature the memory of objects previously experienced, the knowledge born of contact between sense and object, and also their loss. Through all the Vedas I alone am to be known in the form of their respective deities. I am the founder of the Vedanta tradition — the guru who bestows knowledge — and I alone am the true knower of the Veda's meaning.
bhakti.divergence_note: Sridhara: 'samyag antaryamiirupena pravistho 'ham... mattas caiva hetoh pranimantrasya purvanubhutartha-vishaya smritir bhavati... tat-tat-devatadi-rupena aham eva vedyah' — balanced, non-partisan reading that neither collapses to jnana alone nor to exclusive bhakti.

advaita-bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita-bhakti.english_rendering: I, as the Atman of all beings from Brahma down to the immovable, am lodged in the buddhi — as the shruti confirms: 'sa esa iha pravistah.' From Me as Atman arises memory of this life's experience (and, for yogins, of prior births too); from Me arises knowledge through sense-contact; and for those whose minds are agitated by desire, anger, or grief, even the loss of memory and knowledge is from Me. All the Vedas, even those illuminating Indra and the other deities, point only to Me, for 'ekam sad vipra bahudha vadanti.' I am the propagator of the Vedanta tradition as Vyasa and its like, and I alone truly know every layer of the Veda — mantra, Brahmana, upasana, and jnana-kanda.
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: Madhusudana integrates two shruti citations (Chandogya 6.3.2 'anena jivennatmana anuprivisya' and the Rigveda 1.164.46 'ekam sad vipra') to unify the jiva-Atman identity with devotional address; his note that yogins remember past-life objects is a distinctive expansion absent in Shankara and Sridhara.

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: If the Lord is the source of both memory and its loss (apohana / forgetting), what does that imply about the moral status of forgetting — is ignorance a punishment, a protection, or a mercy?
Ramanuja distinguishes three epistemic operations (smriti, jnana, uha / inferential readiness) as separately sourced in the Lord. Does this make epistemology itself a theological discipline?
Madhva uses this verse primarily to establish that Vishnu authored the Vedanta-sutra. What is at stake doctrinally in the question of who authored the foundational sutra?
Madhusudana adds that yogins recover past-life memories from this same source. What does that imply about the continuity of the self across lives if the Lord is the single repository?

Vallabha insists that apohana (the removal of knowledge) is jiva-kalpita (constructed by the jiva) in one reading. Does placing the blame for ignorance entirely on the jiva relieve or increase the practitioner's burden?

All six schools agree that the Vedas 'know' the Lord even when they explicitly name other deities. What interpretive principle licenses this move, and does it strengthen or weaken polytheistic readings of the tradition?
Shankara ties the apportioning of memory and forgetting strictly to karma. Does this make cognition a moral phenomenon — and if so, what follows for education, memory training, or cognitive decline?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you notice that a concept you once understood clearly has become opaque — you cannot recall a name, a skill goes stiff, insight fades — Shankara's reading invites you to locate the cause in prior-action residue rather than in the intellect's failure alone. The practice is not to strain after the memory but to reduce the karma-density that obscures the Atman's natural light through dispassion (vairagya).
everyday_applications.visistadvaita: Ramanuja's antaryamin (inner-ruler) framing means that every flash of good judgment — the moment you sense whether an argument is worth pursuing before working it out — is the Lord's governance operating through you. The Vaishnavite application: treat your own clarity of mind as a form of the Lord's kainkarya (service gift) and your confusion as an invitation to surrender the controlling intellect.
everyday_applications.dvaita: For a Madhva practitioner, the dependence of memory and knowledge on Hari's dispensation is felt most concretely in the context of scripture study: no passage of the Veda 'opens' by personal effort alone. The daily practice is to begin every study session with an explicit acknowledgment of the Lord as the Vedanta-author, invoking His will before presuming comprehension.
everyday_applications.shuddhadvaita: Vallabha's insistence that the removal of knowledge is jiva-kalpita (the jiva's own superimposition) points to a distinctly Pushtimarga application: do not attribute your spiritual forgetfulness to God's will or to karma's impersonal mechanics. The obstruction is your own contracted self-sense. The remedy is seva (devotional service) as an act of self-dissolution, not as earning divine favor.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Sridhara's balanced reading — the Lord as guru who bestows and withholds understanding — maps cleanly onto the traditional teacher-student relationship. The everyday implication: seek a teacher not merely as a conveyor of information but as a living node in the same antaryamin circuit. The right guru does not add knowledge so much as remove the obstruction that prevents the knowledge already lodged in you from surfacing.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusudana's detail that yogins recover past-life memories from this source suggests a gradient of access rather than a binary know-or-forget. In contemporary terms: contemplative practice (dhyana / meditation) is not building new cognitive capacity so much as thinning the veil between the Atman's total memory and the jiva's current access window. Consistency of practice expands the window; emotional agitation (kama, krodha, shoka) contracts it.
